title: Learning Diary
text:
About
Hi, welcome to my Learning Diary for Remotely Sensing City and Environments module! This module is part of the Bartlett Centre for Advanced Spatial Analysis (CASA), University College London (UCL) master’s level course in Urban Spatial Science.
My name is Hania and I am aspiring urban spatial scientist, but my background is in physics and arts. I am passionate about data visualisation and experimenting with different ways to communicate spatial analysis topics to broader audience.
Enjoy earth observation insights, remote sensing techniques and some infographics!

title: 2  Week 2
text: Here are the slides for Week 2:

title: 3  Corrections for Remote Sensing
text:
3.1 Filtering, Texture Analysis and Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
When dealing with satellite data, in most cases, we will deal with pre-processed imagery, which is ready to analyse. There is number of remote sensing data corrections and in this section I would like to introduce few of them, with a main focus on specific ratio index - Normalised Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI).
Before focusing on NDVI, I explored several image processing techniques introduced in the practical, including filtering, texture analysis and Principal Component Analysis (PCA). Visually, this did not necessarily “improve” the image, but it reduces small-scale pixel noise and stabilises local variation. This is particularly relevant when performing further statistical operations (e.g. texture or PCA), as these methods can otherwise be overly sensitive to abrupt pixel-to-pixel differences that may reflect sensor noise rather than real landscape structure. I then calculated GLCM texture (homogeneity) using a 7×7 window. Unlike spectral bands, texture does not measure reflectance but rather the spatial arrangement of pixel values. High homogeneity indicates uniform surfaces (e.g. large vegetated patches or water bodies), while lower values suggest more heterogeneous urban fabric. However, texture outputs are less intuitive to interpret visually and highly dependent on chosen parameters (window size and grey level quantisation). Finally, PCA was applied to the stacked spectral and texture layers to reduce redundancy between correlated bands. PCA transforms the data into new components (PC1, PC2, etc.) that capture decreasing proportions of overall variance. While PCA can be useful for dimensionality reduction and classification, the resulting components are abstract combinations of original variables and therefore less directly interpretable than NDVI. Overall, these steps demonstrated how preprocessing choices influence both visual outputs and statistical stability, even if their interpretability is less straightforward than vegetation indices.

title: 3  Corrections for Remote Sensing
text:
3.2 The Normalised Different Vegetation Index (NDVI)








London NDVI Map







Warsaw NDVI Map






Figure 3.1: Side-by-side spatial analysis of vegetation index. Click either image to expand and compare scales.



My initial spin on practical content came from genuine interest in comparison of green coverage between London and Warsaw. I started off with purely looking at the visualisation of NDVI for both cities wondering if there are significant differences visible to the naked eye. For both cities I used imagery from May 2024, to minimise differences which might be caused by seasonal variations. The minimum of NDVI value was chosen based on fact that for urban areas NDVI > 0.2 is considered to be threshold for “some” vegetation. Maximum NDVI was set to be 0.6, as for London NDVI > 0.6 is less than 0.001% of the city area, and for Warsaw max NDVI value is 0.58.
Although some differences are visible, visualisations weren’t very informative. I decided to run very basic descriptive statistics (see Table 3.1), which revealed a bit more about cities greenery. Warsaw had a slightly higher percentage of vegetation cover, while London seems to have higher value for max NDVI (as well as slightly higher mean NDVI) indicating spots of more lush greenery in the city. The vegetation cover of 66% and 64% seemed like very high values, and after cross-checking with literature it turned to be way off. According to Mayor of London website London green cover is around 51.7%, while Husqvarna Urban Green Space Insights (HUGSI) website shows that London green space is 41%, and Warsaw 50%.
This variations might be partially due to differences in definitions of green space (maybe different thresholds or not accounting for private gardens or green roofs) , but mostly due to simplicity of the method I used. Firstly Landsat 8 data has resolution of 30 m, while Sentinel 2 (used in HUGSI analysis) has a resolution of 10-20 m. When using Landsat 8 data we are analysing 900 m² pixels, which can lead to eg. having very small, but very healthy green backyard which can push an average NDVI pixel value to be > 0.2, and therefore count the whole pixel as a vegetation.



Table 3.1: NDVI Comparison: Warsaw vs. London

title: 4  RS in Policies- Case Study
text:
4.1 Making Cities Resilient 2030 (MCR2030)
MCR 2030 is an initiative which aims to strengthen local resilience of the cities around the world. It was launched by United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction (UNDRR) in collaboration with partners like Global Resilient Cities Network, ICLEI, C40 Cities Climate Leadership and many others (for full list please see this document), after a success of 10-year (2010-2020) campaign “Making Cities Resilient”. Key goals of this partnership is to help cities transition from raising awareness on local resilience to actually implementing risk-reduction and resilient infrastructure. It helps cities align with Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction, the Paris Agreement, and the Sustainable Development Goals (specifically SDG11).
crumbs: 4  RS in Policies- Case Study

title: 4  RS in Policies- Case Study
text:
4.1.1 Wroclaw as a Resilience Hub
As a case study I decided to choose my hometown- Wroclaw. It’s 4 largest city in Poland, located at southwestern part of the country, on the Odra (Oder) river. It’s location makes it prone to the flooding, and city has experience few of them especially in past 50 years. One of the biggest disasters happened in 1997, when Oder and Morava rivers caused flooding in Central Europe. After 1997 flood Wroclaw’s authorities focused on city’s risk-reduction and Wroclaw has become MCR Resilience Hub in 2022, leading an example of resilient infrastructure development. While preparing material for this entry I realised that, because Wroclaw has a solid experience in improving its infrastructure, it’s local authorities are already using remote sensing and spatial analysis in implementing those changes. In case of flooding there is also a well-functioning system in place - Copernicus Emergency Management Service (CEMS), which provides on demand mapping in case of natural hazards.






Figure 4.1: On Demand Mapping on CEMS website- case of floods in South West Poland in 2024.



I started thinking maybe there is no much space for suggestions how to use earth observation in supporting MCR2030-Wroclaw seems to be greener and more resilient to flooding. However, resilience does not stop at administrative borders, and a city’s safety is often dictated by the land-use choices of its immediate neighbours.
crumbs: 4  RS in Policies- Case Study

title: 4  RS in Policies- Case Study
text:
4.2 Resilient infrastructure in suburban areas
While Wroclaw strengthens its internal defenses, the rapid expansion of the surrounding functional urban area presents a new set of vulnerabilities. What about suburban areas which don’t fall under the boundaries of cities? In polish administrative system they are separate entities, with their own mayors, but often falls into bigger agglomeration (like in case of Wroclaw). Being in an agglomeration would mean some degree of collaboration between city president (in Poland cities has presidents, and municipalities has mayors) and mayors of surrounding municipalities, but there is no rigours rules regarding the urban development of city vs its suburban areas. This leads to the situations like Wroclaw case where there are years of efforts to re-green city, making it more resilient, but just outside its boundaries there is rapid urban sprawl happening, with developments built on potential flood zones and intense “soil sealing” with no supporting drainage systems. My suggestion would be not only regarding the use of RS in urban planning but also to create a stronger administrative body responsible of more sustainable (and probably more controlled) development of suburban areas.
crumbs: 4  RS in Policies- Case Study

title: 4  RS in Policies- Case Study
section: 4.3 Proposed Improvement: The Agglomeration Resilience Authority
text:
4.3 Proposed Improvement: The Agglomeration Resilience Authority
To address the disconnect between city planning and suburban sprawl, I suggest the establishment of an Agglomeration Resilience Authority. This would serve as a supra-municipal governing body empowered to oversee land-use permits across the entire metropolitan region. Its primary role would be to ensure that local suburban developments do not compromise regional flood safety or ecological health. By using Remote Sensing (RS) as its primary evidence-based tool, this authority could make informed, objective decisions in the following ways:

Standardizing “Soil Sealing” Limits: By using High-Resolution Multispectral Imagery (e.g., Sentinel-2) to calculate the Normalized Difference Built-up Index (NDBI), the authority can set strict “permeability quotas.” This prevents excessive concrete cover in the suburbs from increasing runoff and overwhelming Wroclaw’s drainage systems.
Dynamic Flood Risk Mapping: Utilizing LiDAR-derived Digital Elevation Models (DEMs) allows for the identification of natural drainage corridors and micro-depressions. The authority can use this data to veto “high-risk” developments in areas that RS identifies as natural water retention zones, even if they aren’t marked on outdated traditional maps.
Monitoring Trans-boundary Green Belts: Using the Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI), the body can ensure that “Green Lungs” remain continuous across administrative borders. RS allows for real-time tracking of whether suburban encroachment is fragmenting the forests and wetlands that the entire agglomeration relies on for cooling and water absorption.
crumbs: 4  RS in Policies- Case Study

title: 4  RS in Policies- Case Study
text:
4.4 Conclusion: Data-Driven Solidarity
By integrating Remote Sensing into a unified administrative framework, we move from “isolated resilience” to “territorial resilience.” This approach directly supports the MCR2030 goal of transitioning from awareness to implementation by ensuring urban growth is monitored through the lens of environmental physics rather than political boundaries. For Wroclaw to remain a true Resilience Hub, it must look beyond its city signs and use Earth Observation to lead its entire metropolitan region toward a safer, more porous future. This data-driven solidarity ensures that a win for one municipality is not a disaster for its neighbour.
crumbs: 4  RS in Policies- Case Study
